items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: Alla prima paintings are normally finished:
items.why: It's In one wet-on-wet sitting. “Over many days of glazes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is most likely to be a warm colour?
items.why: Correct answer: Cadmium red. “Cobalt blue” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many primary colours form the basis of mixing in paint?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
